objects.name: Orion Nebula
objects.description: One of the brightest nebulae visible to the naked eye, containing a young open cluster and serving as a prolific stellar nursery roughly 1,344 light-years away.

objects.name: Crab Nebula
objects.description: The remnant of a supernova recorded by Chinese astronomers in 1054 AD, powered by a rapidly spinning neutron star at its center.

objects.name: Lagoon Nebula
objects.description: A giant interstellar cloud in Sagittarius, one of only two star-forming nebulae faintly visible to the naked eye from mid-northern latitudes.

objects.name: Omega Nebula
objects.description: Also known as the Swan or Horseshoe Nebula, this luminous H II region in Sagittarius contains an open cluster of 35 hot young stars.

objects.name: Trifid Nebula
objects.description: A combination of emission, reflection, and dark nebula divided into three lobes by obscuring dust lanes.

objects.name: Dumbbell Nebula
objects.description: The first planetary nebula ever discovered, found by Charles Messier in 1764, showing a bright bi-lobed structure.

objects.name: Ring Nebula
objects.description: A well-known planetary nebula in Lyra whose ring-shaped appearance is a projection effect of a barrel-shaped shell of glowing gas.

objects.name: Owl Nebula
objects.description: A planetary nebula in Ursa Major named for two dark circular patches that give it an owl-like appearance.

objects.name: Helix Nebula
objects.description: One of the nearest and largest planetary nebulae, sometimes called the Eye of God due to its striking appearance.

objects.name: North America Nebula
objects.description: A large emission nebula in Cygnus whose shape resembles the continent of North America, particularly the Gulf of Mexico region.

objects.name: Pelican Nebula
objects.description: An emission nebula adjacent to the North America Nebula, separated by a molecular cloud and shaped like a pelican.

objects.name: Veil Nebula
objects.description: A large supernova remnant in Cygnus from a star that exploded roughly 10,000 to 20,000 years ago, forming delicate filaments of shocked gas.

objects.name: Rosette Nebula
objects.description: A large spherical H II region in Monoceros, with the open cluster NGC 2244 at its center whose stellar winds created the central cavity.

objects.name: Eagle Nebula
objects.description: Famous for the Pillars of Creation photographed by the Hubble Space Telescope, this emission nebula hosts active star formation.

objects.name: Eta Carinae Nebula
objects.description: One of the largest and brightest nebulae in the sky, home to the massive and unstable star Eta Carinae and the Keyhole Nebula.

objects.name: Horsehead Nebula
objects.description: One of the most identifiable dark nebulae, its distinctive horse-head shape is silhouetted against the bright emission nebula IC 434.

objects.name: Eskimo Nebula
objects.description: A planetary nebula in Gemini with a complex double-shell structure resembling a face surrounded by a fur parka hood.

objects.name: Cat's Eye Nebula
objects.description: One of the most structurally complex planetary nebulae known, with concentric rings, jets, and knots revealing multiple episodes of mass ejection.

objects.name: Blinking Planetary
objects.description: A planetary nebula in Cygnus that appears to blink on and off as the observer alternates between direct and averted vision.

objects.name: Eight-Burst Nebula
objects.description: Also called the Southern Ring Nebula, JWST observations revealed at least two stars shaped this nebula's intricate ring structure.

objects.name: NGC 2440
objects.description: A planetary nebula in Puppis whose central star is one of the hottest white dwarfs known, with a surface temperature exceeding 200,000 K.

objects.name: Butterfly Nebula
objects.description: A bipolar planetary nebula in Scorpius with a wingspan of over 3 light-years, ejected by one of the hottest known central stars.

objects.name: NGC 7027
objects.description: One of the youngest and brightest planetary nebulae, only about 600 years old, with a complex multipolar structure.

objects.name: Spirograph Nebula
objects.description: A young planetary nebula in Lepus with intricate patterns in its glowing gas that resemble a spirograph drawing.

objects.name: Tarantula Nebula
objects.description: The most luminous H II region in the Local Group, located in the Large Magellanic Cloud and home to the massive star cluster R136.

objects.name: NGC 604
objects.description: One of the largest H II regions known, located in the Triangulum Galaxy M33 and spanning roughly 1,500 light-years across.

objects.name: De Mairan's Nebula
objects.description: A smaller H II region separated from the main Orion Nebula by a dark dust lane, illuminated by the star NU Orionis.

objects.name: California Nebula
objects.description: A large emission nebula in Perseus whose shape resembles the US state of California, ionized by the hot star Xi Persei.

objects.name: Flame Nebula
objects.description: An emission nebula in Orion near Alnitak, whose dark dust lanes create a flame-like pattern against the bright hydrogen glow.

objects.name: Cocoon Nebula
objects.description: A compact emission and reflection nebula in Cygnus at the end of a long dark nebula known as Barnard 168.

objects.name: Bubble Nebula
objects.description: An emission nebula in Cassiopeia formed by the stellar wind from a massive O-type star inflating a shell of ionized gas.

objects.name: Crescent Nebula
objects.description: An emission nebula in Cygnus formed by the fast stellar wind of the Wolf-Rayet star WR 136 colliding with slower material ejected earlier.

objects.name: Thor's Helmet
objects.description: A distinctive emission nebula in Canis Major shaped like a winged helmet, blown by the energetic Wolf-Rayet star WR 7.

objects.name: Cat's Paw Nebula
objects.description: A vast emission nebula in Scorpius whose reddish paw-print pattern is produced by ionized hydrogen in one of the most active nurseries in the Milky Way.

objects.name: NGC 3603
objects.description: One of the most massive visible H II regions in the Milky Way, containing a compact starburst cluster with some of the most massive stars known.

objects.name: Seagull Nebula
objects.description: A large emission nebula on the border of Monoceros and Canis Major whose shape resembles a seagull in flight.

objects.name: War and Peace Nebula
objects.description: A diffuse emission nebula in Scorpius containing several massive star clusters and pillars of creation-like structures sculpted by intense radiation.

objects.name: NGC 1491
objects.description: A bright emission nebula in Perseus illuminated by the hot blue star BD+50 886, surrounded by dark molecular clouds.

objects.name: Pacman Nebula
objects.description: An H II region in Cassiopeia named for its resemblance to the Pac-Man character, with prominent Bok globules and active star formation.

objects.name: Barnard 68
objects.description: A nearby isolated dark molecular cloud so dense that no background stars are visible through its center, yet remarkably well-defined against the starfield.

objects.name: Snake Nebula
objects.description: A sinuous dark nebula in Ophiuchus that winds through the star clouds like a snake, part of the Dark Horse complex.

objects.name: Coalsack Nebula
objects.description: The most prominent dark nebula in the sky, visible to the naked eye as a dark patch in the southern Milky Way near the Southern Cross.

objects.name: Running Man Nebula
objects.description: A reflection nebula just north of the Orion Nebula whose dust patterns suggest a running human figure in deep photographs.

objects.name: M78
objects.description: The brightest diffuse reflection nebula in the sky, illuminated by two B-type stars and located in the Orion molecular cloud complex.

objects.name: Witch Head Nebula
objects.description: A faint reflection nebula in Eridanus illuminated by the nearby supergiant star Rigel, whose shape suggests a witch's profile.

objects.name: NGC 1333
objects.description: A young reflection nebula in Perseus that is one of the nearest active star-forming regions, containing numerous Herbig-Haro objects.

objects.name: Iris Nebula
objects.description: A bright reflection nebula in Cepheus surrounded by a large dusty region, illuminated by the young star HD 200775.

objects.name: Little Dumbbell Nebula
objects.description: A bipolar planetary nebula in Perseus that is one of the faintest Messier objects, resembling a miniature version of M27.

objects.name: NGC 6781
objects.description: A large planetary nebula in Aquila with a barrel-shaped structure seen nearly pole-on, giving it a distinctive cylindrical appearance.

objects.name: Sh2-106
objects.description: A compact bipolar emission nebula in Cygnus shaped like an hourglass, with a massive young star at its narrow waist driving powerful outflows.

objects.name: Great Globular Cluster in Hercules
objects.description: One of the best-known globular clusters in the northern sky, containing several hundred thousand stars packed into a region about 145 light-years across.

objects.name: M3
objects.description: One of the largest and brightest globular clusters, containing the most variable stars of any known cluster with over 270 identified.

objects.name: M5
objects.description: One of the oldest globular clusters at roughly 13 billion years, containing over 100 known variable stars and a millisecond pulsar.

objects.name: M15
objects.description: One of the densest globular clusters known, possibly harboring a central intermediate-mass black hole and containing the planetary nebula Pease 1.

objects.name: M22
objects.description: One of the nearest globular clusters to Earth and one of the first to be discovered, notable for containing two stellar-mass black holes.

objects.name: M4
objects.description: The nearest globular cluster to Earth, located adjacent to Antares, and the first globular in which individual stars were resolved.

objects.name: M2
objects.description: One of the largest known globular clusters spanning about 175 light-years, noted for its rich and compact core.

objects.name: M92
objects.description: One of the oldest and brightest globular clusters in the Milky Way, often overlooked due to its proximity to the more famous M13.

objects.name: M80
objects.description: A dense globular cluster in Scorpius where a nova was observed in 1860, one of only two novae ever seen in a globular cluster.

objects.name: Omega Centauri
objects.description: The largest and most massive globular cluster in the Milky Way, with roughly ten million stars, suspected to be the remnant nucleus of a dwarf galaxy.

objects.name: 47 Tucanae
objects.description: The second brightest globular cluster after Omega Centauri, famous for its dense core and large population of millisecond pulsars.

objects.name: NGC 6752
objects.description: The third brightest globular cluster in the sky, one of the nearest, containing a large population of blue stragglers in its core.

objects.name: M55
objects.description: A large but loosely concentrated globular cluster in Sagittarius, relatively easy to resolve into individual stars due to its open structure.

objects.name: M10
objects.description: A globular cluster in Ophiuchus slightly closer and brighter than its neighbor M12, with a moderately dense core.

objects.name: M12
objects.description: A globular cluster in Ophiuchus with an unusually low proportion of low-mass stars, likely stripped by tidal interactions with the Milky Way.

objects.name: M107
objects.description: One of the more open and less concentrated globular clusters, making individual stars relatively easy to resolve even in moderate telescopes.

objects.name: Palomar 5
objects.description: A sparse and disrupted globular cluster famous for its spectacular tidal tails stretching over 30,000 light-years, revealing ongoing tidal stripping.

objects.name: Terzan 7
objects.description: A globular cluster associated with the Sagittarius Dwarf Elliptical Galaxy, being accreted by the Milky Way along with its parent galaxy.

objects.name: M79
objects.description: An unusually isolated globular cluster in Lepus, far from the galactic center, thought to have been captured from the Canis Major Dwarf Galaxy.

objects.name: NGC 2808
objects.description: A massive globular cluster with at least three distinct generations of stars, challenging traditional models of single-burst globular cluster formation.

objects.name: Pleiades
objects.description: The most famous open cluster in the sky, known since antiquity, containing hot blue stars surrounded by faint reflection nebulosity.

objects.name: Hyades
objects.description: The nearest open cluster to the Sun and one of the best-studied, forming the distinctive V-shape of the head of Taurus.

objects.name: Praesepe
objects.description: One of the nearest open clusters, visible to the naked eye as a misty patch in Cancer, known since antiquity as the Beehive.

objects.name: Double Cluster
objects.description: A pair of open clusters in Perseus visible to the naked eye, each containing several hundred young blue-white supergiants.

objects.name: Wild Duck Cluster
objects.description: One of the richest and most compact open clusters known, whose fan-shaped outline resembles a flock of wild ducks in flight.

objects.name: Jewel Box Cluster
objects.description: A young open cluster near Beta Crucis containing stars of contrasting colors — blue, white, and a prominent red supergiant — resembling scattered jewels.

objects.name: Butterfly Cluster
objects.description: An open cluster in Scorpius whose pattern of stars resembles the outline of a butterfly with open wings.

objects.name: Ptolemy's Cluster
objects.description: A large bright open cluster recorded by Ptolemy in 130 AD, one of the most prominent clusters visible to the naked eye.

objects.name: M35
objects.description: A rich open cluster in Gemini visible to the naked eye, with the much more distant cluster NGC 2158 appearing nearby in the same field of view.

objects.name: M37
objects.description: The richest and brightest of the three open clusters in Auriga, containing about a dozen red giants among its hundreds of members.

objects.name: M36
objects.description: A compact open cluster in Auriga with a handful of bright B-type stars, resembling a scaled-down version of the Pleiades.

objects.name: M38
objects.description: An open cluster in Auriga whose brightest stars form a distinctive cross or oblique pi pattern.

objects.name: Wishing Well Cluster
objects.description: A rich open cluster in Carina described by John Herschel as the finest cluster in the sky, and the first target observed by the Hubble Space Telescope.

objects.name: M67
objects.description: One of the oldest known open clusters at about 4 billion years, with a chemical composition remarkably similar to the Sun.

objects.name: Southern Pleiades
objects.description: A bright open cluster in Carina centered on the third-magnitude star Theta Carinae, easily visible to the naked eye from the Southern Hemisphere.

objects.name: Sagittarius A*
objects.description: The supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way, imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2022 with a mass of four million Suns.

objects.name: Cygnus X-1
objects.description: The first widely accepted stellar-mass black hole, a high-mass X-ray binary whose identification settled a famous bet between Stephen Hawking and Kip Thorne.

objects.name: V404 Cygni
objects.description: A low-mass X-ray binary that underwent dramatic outbursts in 1989 and 2015, providing some of the best dynamical mass measurements for a stellar black hole.

objects.name: A0620-00
objects.description: A soft X-ray transient that produced the brightest X-ray outburst ever recorded in 1975, containing one of the nearest known black holes.

objects.name: GRS 1915+105
objects.description: The first Galactic source found to exhibit apparent superluminal jet motion, with highly variable X-ray emission showing over a dozen distinct patterns.

objects.name: LMC X-3
objects.description: One of the first stellar-mass black hole candidates identified, located in the Large Magellanic Cloud in a persistent X-ray binary system.

objects.name: GX 339-4
objects.description: A recurrent X-ray transient that has undergone numerous outbursts, serving as a key laboratory for studying black hole accretion state transitions.

objects.name: XTE J1118+480
objects.description: A black hole X-ray binary at high galactic latitude, possibly originating from the galactic halo or kicked out of the disk by a supernova.

objects.name: M87*
objects.description: The supermassive black hole at the center of the giant elliptical galaxy M87, the first black hole ever directly imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019.

objects.name: SS 433
objects.description: A remarkable eclipsing X-ray binary with precessing relativistic jets moving at 26% the speed of light, embedded within the supernova remnant W50.

objects.name: Crab Pulsar
objects.description: A young and energetic pulsar at the heart of the Crab Nebula, one of the few pulsars detectable across the entire electromagnetic spectrum.

objects.name: Vela Pulsar
objects.description: One of the nearest and brightest pulsars, embedded in the Vela supernova remnant and famous for exhibiting large glitches in its rotation period.

objects.name: PSR B1919+21
objects.description: The first pulsar ever discovered, found by Jocelyn Bell Burnell in 1967, initially designated LGM-1 for Little Green Men.

objects.name: PSR J0437-4715
objects.description: The nearest and brightest millisecond pulsar, extensively used for pulsar timing array gravitational wave detection experiments.

objects.name: PSR B1257+12
objects.description: A millisecond pulsar orbited by the first confirmed exoplanets ever discovered, announced in 1992, predating the first main-sequence exoplanet discovery.

objects.name: Geminga
objects.description: A nearby gamma-ray pulsar whose name is a play on a Milanese dialect phrase meaning 'it's not there,' reflecting its initial lack of radio emission.

objects.name: PSR J0030+0451
objects.description: A millisecond pulsar whose X-ray hot spots were mapped by NICER, providing the first precise measurement of a neutron star's size and shape.

objects.name: PSR J1614-2230
objects.description: A massive neutron star whose precise mass measurement of nearly two solar masses ruled out many soft equations of state for nuclear matter.

objects.name: PSR J0348+0432
objects.description: A massive pulsar in a compact orbit with a white dwarf, providing stringent tests of general relativity in the strong-field regime.

objects.name: PSR J0737-3039A
objects.description: The only known double pulsar system, providing the most precise tests of general relativity in strong gravitational fields to date.

objects.name: PSR B1937+21
objects.description: The first millisecond pulsar discovered in 1982, spinning at 642 revolutions per second, which held the fastest pulsar record for 24 years.

objects.name: PSR J1748-2446ad
objects.description: The fastest-spinning known pulsar at 716 Hz, located in the globular cluster Terzan 5, with its equator moving at nearly 24% the speed of light.

objects.name: PSR J0205+6449
objects.description: A young pulsar at the center of the supernova remnant 3C 58, possibly associated with the historical supernova of 1181 AD recorded by Chinese and Japanese astronomers.

objects.name: PSR B1509-58
objects.description: A young X-ray pulsar producing a spectacular pulsar wind nebula imaged by Chandra, whose shape resembles a cosmic hand reaching across space.

objects.name: PSR J2124-3358
objects.description: An isolated millisecond pulsar with a distinctive bow-shock nebula created by its high-velocity motion through the interstellar medium.

objects.name: PSR J1023+0038
objects.description: A transitional millisecond pulsar that switches between accretion-powered and rotation-powered states, providing a direct link between X-ray binaries and millisecond pulsars.

objects.name: PSR B0656+14
objects.description: A middle-aged pulsar in the Monogem Ring supernova remnant, one of the few pulsars detected in optical, X-ray, and gamma-ray wavelengths.

objects.name: PSR J1846-0258
objects.description: A young high-magnetic-field pulsar in the supernova remnant Kes 75 that exhibited magnetar-like bursts in 2006, blurring the line between pulsars and magnetars.

objects.name: PSR B0950+08
objects.description: One of the nearest and first pulsars discovered, notable for giant pulse emission and serving as an important calibrator for interstellar dispersion studies.

objects.name: PSR J1740-5340
objects.description: A millisecond pulsar in the globular cluster NGC 6397 in an eclipsing binary with a bloated red companion, regularly obscured by the companion's wind.

objects.name: SGR 1806-20
objects.description: Source of the most energetic gamma-ray flare ever recorded from within the Milky Way on December 27, 2004, briefly outshining the entire galaxy.

objects.name: SGR 1900+14
objects.description: A soft gamma repeater that produced a giant flare in 1998 strong enough to ionize Earth's upper atmosphere from 20,000 light-years away.

objects.name: 1E 2259+586
objects.description: An anomalous X-ray pulsar in the supernova remnant CTB 109 that exhibited an anti-glitch in 2012 — a sudden spin-down rather than a spin-up.

objects.name: 4U 0142+61
objects.description: An anomalous X-ray pulsar that was the first magnetar found to emit in the optical and infrared, possibly surrounded by a passive debris disk.

objects.name: SGR 0526-66
objects.description: The source of the first observed giant magnetar flare on March 5, 1979, located in the supernova remnant N49 in the Large Magellanic Cloud.

objects.name: 1E 1048.1-5937
objects.description: An isolated anomalous X-ray pulsar that has shown both gradual and sudden changes in its spin-down rate, along with variable X-ray flux.

objects.name: SGR J1745-2900
objects.description: A magnetar located only 0.1 parsecs from the supermassive black hole Sagittarius A*, providing a unique probe of the extreme Galactic center environment.

objects.name: CXOU J164710.2-455216
objects.description: A magnetar in the massive star cluster Westerlund 1, challenging formation models as its progenitor star must have been over 40 solar masses.

objects.name: XTE J1810-197
objects.description: The first transient anomalous X-ray pulsar discovered and the first magnetar from which radio pulsations were detected.

objects.name: Swift J1818.0-1607
objects.description: One of the youngest known magnetars at roughly 240 years old, and one of the fastest-spinning magnetars ever discovered.

objects.name: Algol
objects.description: The 'Demon Star,' the best-known eclipsing binary whose regular brightness variations were likely known to ancient Egyptian astronomers 3,000 years ago.

objects.name: Mira
objects.description: The prototype long-period variable star, discovered in 1596, trailing a comet-like tail of material 13 light-years long revealed by ultraviolet observations.

objects.name: Delta Cephei
objects.description: The prototype Cepheid variable whose period-luminosity relationship became the cornerstone for measuring cosmic distances.

objects.name: RR Lyrae
objects.description: The prototype of RR Lyrae variables, pulsating giant stars used as standard candles to measure distances to globular clusters and nearby galaxies.

objects.name: Eta Carinae
objects.description: One of the most massive and luminous stars known, which underwent a dramatic Great Eruption in the 1840s that briefly made it the second-brightest star in the sky.

objects.name: Betelgeuse
objects.description: A red supergiant approaching the end of its life, whose unexpected dimming in late 2019 was caused by a massive surface eruption and resulting dust cloud.

objects.name: Hind's Crimson Star
objects.description: One of the reddest stars visible in amateur telescopes, a carbon star whose deep crimson color intensifies near minimum brightness.

objects.name: Chi Cygni
objects.description: A Mira-type variable with one of the largest brightness ranges of any pulsating star, varying by nearly 11 magnitudes over its 408-day cycle.

objects.name: R Coronae Borealis
objects.description: The prototype of a rare class of hydrogen-poor carbon stars that undergo sudden, unpredictable fadings when carbon dust condenses in the line of sight.

objects.name: T Tauri
objects.description: The prototype of pre-main-sequence stars still contracting toward the main sequence, surrounded by a protoplanetary disk and driving bipolar jets.

objects.name: FU Orionis
objects.description: The prototype of FUor variables that undergo dramatic brightenings caused by sudden increases in accretion rate from their circumstellar disks.

objects.name: SS Cygni
objects.description: The brightest dwarf nova in the sky and one of the most studied cataclysmic variables, undergoing outbursts roughly every 50 days.

objects.name: U Geminorum
objects.description: The prototype dwarf nova, first observed in outburst in 1855, consisting of a white dwarf accreting material from a red dwarf companion.

objects.name: Beta Lyrae
objects.description: The prototype of a class of semi-detached eclipsing binaries where mass transfer between the stars produces a continuously varying light curve.

objects.name: W Ursae Majoris
objects.description: The prototype of contact binary systems where both stars overflow their Roche lobes and share a common envelope of material.

objects.name: RS Canum Venaticorum
objects.description: The prototype of chromospherically active binary stars exhibiting large starspots, strong calcium emission, and frequent flaring activity.

objects.name: BY Draconis
objects.description: The prototype of spotted variable stars, a nearby red dwarf whose brightness varies as large cool starspots rotate in and out of view.

objects.name: R Aquarii
objects.description: A symbiotic binary combining a Mira variable and a white dwarf, producing a spectacular jet-like nebula from material accreted onto the white dwarf.

objects.name: Polaris
objects.description: The current North Star and the nearest Cepheid variable, whose pulsation amplitude has been increasing after nearly ceasing in the 1990s.

objects.name: R Scuti
objects.description: The brightest RV Tauri variable, a pulsating post-AGB star exhibiting alternating deep and shallow minima in its light curve.

objects.name: S Doradus
objects.description: One of the most luminous stars known, the prototype of S Doradus variables that exhibit irregular eruptions, located in the Large Magellanic Cloud.

objects.name: P Cygni
objects.description: A luminous blue variable whose distinctive spectral profile — emission with a blueshifted absorption component — is now named after it.

objects.name: AG Carinae
objects.description: One of the most luminous stars in the Milky Way, a luminous blue variable surrounded by a spectacular ring nebula of ejected material.

objects.name: WR 104
objects.description: A Wolf-Rayet star producing a spectacular rotating pinwheel nebula of dust, wound up by the orbital motion of its binary companion.

objects.name: V838 Monocerotis
objects.description: A star that underwent an unusual outburst in 2002 producing a spectacular light echo expanding through surrounding dust, whose mechanism remains debated.
